label: delphi-r2-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 78,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a reviewing court finds both halves of the proposition true: the Signal content is \"public records\" under San Jose because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing) regardless of the personal device/app used, and the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer \u2014 reached by searching only city systems despite a request explicitly covering \"any platform or account\" and despite ~40 screenshots known to still exist \u2014 falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard San Jose contemplates, making the response a CPRA violation. The weaker link is the second prong, since San Jose expressly left search methodology for remand rather than adopting a bright-line duty-to-inquire rule, so a court could excuse a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the group at the time of the initial response.",
  "rationales": [
    "San Jose holds that writings concerning the conduct of public business do not lose their public character merely because they were sent or received on a personal account, and the Signal content described (franchise terms, diversion targets, what council will accept, RFP timing) is squarely business-related rather than personal or purely political chatter.",
    "Because three of five councilmembers \u2014 acting as public officials on a matter of pending city business \u2014 participated, the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' element of Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.530 (former \u00a7 6252(e)) is satisfied under San Jose's agency-law reasoning regardless of the device or app used.",
    "Pacific Refuse's request expressly asked for records 'on any platform or account,' giving the clerk direct notice that personal-account communications were in scope, so confining the search to city systems alone is unlikely to satisfy the 'reasonably calculated to uncover all responsive documents' standard courts apply to CPRA search adequacy.",
    "At least ~40 screenshots indisputably still exist in a councilmember's possession and were apparently never sought, so 'no responsive records' was not merely under-researched but arguably factually false as to existing, retrievable records \u2014 a violation independent of the auto-deletion issue.",
    "Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly remanded rather than resolving search-methodology questions, so whether a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response had an affirmative duty to inquire of each councilmember is a genuinely unsettled application of the doctrine, which could support a good-faith-search defense and lower the probability the response is deemed an outright violation."
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court treats the clerk's apparent lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of the CPRA response as excusing (versus aggravating) the failure to inquire of individual councilmembers, since San Jose did not adopt a bright-line duty-to-ask rule and left search-adequacy standards for case-by-case development.",
  "authorities": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Cal. Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.530 (former \u00a7 6252(e)) \u2014 definition of 'public records'",
    "Cal. Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.545 (former \u00a7 6252(g)) \u2014 definition of 'writing'",
    "Cal. Gov. Code \u00a7 7922.530 (former \u00a7 6253(a)) \u2014 duty to make identifiable, reasonably described records promptly available",
    "American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 \u2014 verify relevance to search-adequacy standard",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 \u2014 local agency records retention/destruction authority (context for related retention-duty question, not the public-records classification itself)"
  ]
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":76,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"A reviewing court more likely than not holds both halves of the proposition: the Signal content is a \"public record\" under San Jose's content-based test because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will accept,\" RFP timing), and the clerk's \"no responsive records\" response — reached by searching only city systems despite a request expressly covering \"any platform or account\" and despite ~40 screenshots known to survive — falls short of a reasonably-calculated search and constitutes a CPRA violation. The weakest link remains prong two, since San Jose left search-methodology standards for case-by-case development, and two additional wrinkles (the third-party/ephemeral-platform character of the channel, and the possibility a court confines any \"public records\" holding to the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus) add genuine, if secondary, uncertainty to prong one that I had previously treated as close to settled.","rationales":["San Jose's content-controls-not-location holding is essentially uncontested across the panel (7/7), and the described subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' RFP timing) is core conduct-of-public-business content, not personal or incidental chatter, so prong one rests on a well-established doctrinal foundation.","Three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — participated in substantive deliberation, which several panelists (and I) treat as reinforcing both the content nexus and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element under San Jose's agency-law reasoning; this is a genuine strength of the classification argument, not merely cumulative detail.","The ~40 surviving screenshots make 'no responsive records' arguably factually false at the moment it was given, independent of the auto-deletion issue — a point 6 of 7 panelists converged on independently, which I treat as a strong, largely uncontested basis for finding a violation on prong two regardless of how the search-methodology dispute is resolved.","Countervailing and largest source of uncertainty: San Jose expressly left search-methodology doctrine for case-by-case development, and the panel split on whether the request's 'any platform or account' language alone triggers a duty to inquire of each custodian or whether the clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response supports a good-faith reasonable-search defense; I cannot resolve this doctrinal fork with confidence, and it is the crux on which prong two turns.","Two panel-raised wrinkles I had underweighted in round 1 modestly increase uncertainty on prong one: (a) the consultant-created, third-party-hosted, disappearing-message character of the channel — distinguishable from San Jose's own facts involving officials' own texts/emails — creates a genuinely open question about whether councilmembers 'used or retained' the writings in the relevant sense; and (b) a court could confine any affirmative 'public records' holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of deleted messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than resolving their public-record status outright, which would only partially validate the proposition as framed."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the CPRA's search-adequacy standard is judged against the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge at the time of response (permitting a good-faith-search defense given no known awareness of the Signal group) or against an objective 'any platform or account' request-scope standard that itself imposes a duty to pre-emptively canvass every named custodian — San Jose left search methodology for case-specific development, and the panel's own citations to post-remand authority (e.g., the 2018 Court of Appeal decision) are disputed and unverified, so this doctrinal fork remains genuinely unresolved.","authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 — verify whether this remand decision actually establishes a duty to inquire of individual custodians about personal-account searches, as one panelist asserted and others disputed","Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'","Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.545 (former § 6252(g)) — definition of 'writing'","Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.530 / § 7922.500 (former § 6253) — duty to make identifiable, reasonably described records promptly available","Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — duty to assist requester in identifying records and overcoming practical basis for denial","American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — verify relevance to search-adequacy standard","AB 700 (Stats. 2021) personal-account search provisions — section number and chapter disputed among panelists (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5); verify correct citation before relying on it","Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority (context for the related retention-duty question, not the public-records classification itself)"],"changed":true,"response_to_panel":"The broad consensus on Themes A, B, D, and F essentially confirmed the structure of my round-1 reasoning and, since I was already at the panel median (78), didn't call for a large revision. What did move me — modestly, down to 76 — was crediting two wrinkles I had previously treated as settled or minor: the third-party/ephemeral-platform distinction raised by the lowest-probability panelist (Theme L), and the possibility that a court confines its 'public records' holding to only the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus (Theme I, raised by the two highest-probability panelists but flagged by me as an underappreciated scope-limiting risk to the proposition as literally worded). Conversely, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the screenshot-falsity point (Theme D) reinforced rather than changed my existing reasoning, so they didn't move the number further. I did not adopt the disputed 2018 Court of Appeal citation (Disagreement point 2) in either direction since I cannot verify its actual holding and the panel itself split on what it stands for; I've flagged it for verification rather than let it push my estimate either way."}

data.position_summary: A reviewing court more likely than not holds both halves of the proposition: the Signal content is a "public record" under San Jose's content-based test because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing), and the clerk's "no responsive records" response — reached by searching only city systems despite a request expressly covering "any platform or account" and despite ~40 screenshots known to survive — falls short of a reasonably-calculated search and constitutes a CPRA violation. The weakest link remains prong two, since San Jose left search-methodology standards for case-by-case development, and two additional wrinkles (the third-party/ephemeral-platform character of the channel, and the possibility a court confines any "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus) add genuine, if secondary, uncertainty to prong one that I had previously treated as close to settled.
rationales: San Jose's content-controls-not-location holding is essentially uncontested across the panel (7/7), and the described subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' RFP timing) is core conduct-of-public-business content, not personal or incidental chatter, so prong one rests on a well-established doctrinal foundation.

Three of five councilmembers — a voting majority — participated in substantive deliberation, which several panelists (and I) treat as reinforcing both the content nexus and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element under San Jose's agency-law reasoning; this is a genuine strength of the classification argument, not merely cumulative detail.

The ~40 surviving screenshots make 'no responsive records' arguably factually false at the moment it was given, independent of the auto-deletion issue — a point 6 of 7 panelists converged on independently, which I treat as a strong, largely uncontested basis for finding a violation on prong two regardless of how the search-methodology dispute is resolved.

Countervailing and largest source of uncertainty: San Jose expressly left search-methodology doctrine for case-by-case development, and the panel split on whether the request's 'any platform or account' language alone triggers a duty to inquire of each custodian or whether the clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response supports a good-faith reasonable-search defense; I cannot resolve this doctrinal fork with confidence, and it is the crux on which prong two turns.

Two panel-raised wrinkles I had underweighted in round 1 modestly increase uncertainty on prong one: (a) the consultant-created, third-party-hosted, disappearing-message character of the channel — distinguishable from San Jose's own facts involving officials' own texts/emails — creates a genuinely open question about whether councilmembers 'used or retained' the writings in the relevant sense; and (b) a court could confine any affirmative 'public records' holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of deleted messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than resolving their public-record status outright, which would only partially validate the proposition as framed.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the CPRA's search-adequacy standard is judged against the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge at the time of response (permitting a good-faith-search defense given no known awareness of the Signal group) or against an objective 'any platform or account' request-scope standard that itself imposes a duty to pre-emptively canvass every named custodian — San Jose left search methodology for case-specific development, and the panel's own citations to post-remand authority (e.g., the 2018 Court of Appeal decision) are disputed and unverified, so this doctrinal fork remains genuinely unresolved.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608

City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 — verify whether this remand decision actually establishes a duty to inquire of individual custodians about personal-account searches, as one panelist asserted and others disputed
Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'
Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.530 / § 7922.500 (former § 6253) — duty to make identifiable, reasonably described records promptly available
Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — duty to assist requester in identifying records and overcoming practical basis for denial
American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — verify relevance to search-adequacy standard

AB 700 (Stats. 2021) personal-account search provisions — section number and chapter disputed among panelists (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5); verify correct citation before relying on it
Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority (context for the related retention-duty question, not the public-records classification itself)
data.response_to_panel: The broad consensus on Themes A, B, D, and F essentially confirmed the structure of my round-1 reasoning and, since I was already at the panel median (78), didn't call for a large revision. What did move me — modestly, down to 76 — was crediting two wrinkles I had previously treated as settled or minor: the third-party/ephemeral-platform distinction raised by the lowest-probability panelist (Theme L), and the possibility that a court confines its 'public records' holding to only the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus (Theme I, raised by the two highest-probability panelists but flagged by me as an underappreciated scope-limiting risk to the proposition as literally worded). Conversely, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the screenshot-falsity point (Theme D) reinforced rather than changed my existing reasoning, so they didn't move the number further. I did not adopt the disputed 2018 Court of Appeal citation (Disagreement point 2) in either direction since I cannot verify its actual holding and the panel itself split on what it stands for; I've flagged it for verification rather than let it push my estimate either way.
